tldr: Headlines frame Argentina's semifinal win as both a Messi-orchestrated comeback and the latest chapter of a culturally charged rivalry: outlets from Australia, Germany, the US, and China foreground 60 years of England-Argentina antagonism in their titles, while South Asian, African, and wire-service outlets lead with Messi as decisive hero. Fan emotion — Buenos Aires celebrations and London crowd disorder — surfaces in a minority of headlines.

evidence.quote: rivalry is one of the ugliest in football
'They hate us and we hate them'
add another chapter to their long-time rivalry
A football rivalry full of history
Old rivals, new battle: Argentina and England clash
'It is crazy': Argentina's Messi adds third final
MAGICAL MESSI: Messi-inspired Argentina rally late
Messi inspires Argentina into second straight World Cup
Messi magic sends Argentina into World Cup final
Messi inspires Argentina's late fightback to sink England
Harry Kane 'gutted' after England crashes out in semis
London police struggle to contain raging England fans

silences.note: IRNA's headline is a purely technical pre-match preview ('team news and predicted lineups') with no framing signal — its title is indistinguishable from a fixture-info page and contributes nothing to any of the three headline frames.

single_outlet_findings.finding: Only headline in the corpus to foreground London fan disorder — 'London police struggle to contain raging England fans' — placing a public-safety story at the title level absent from all other outlets.

bottom_line: The headline layer divides into two registers: a Messi-as-decisive-hero result story dominant across South Asian, African, and wire-service outlets, and a culturally charged rivalry story in outlets from Australia, Germany, the US, and China — with only VnExpress breaking from both to headline crowd disorder in London.
